does: This paper investigates machine unlearning applied to MRI reconstruction, demonstrating that unwanted data samples can be removed from trained models without full retraining. The authors develop a protocol to quantify shared knowledge between organ-specific datasets and show that combining training data from different anatomical regions can introduce hallucinations and quality degradation in reconstructions. They use unlearning specifically to remove these hallucination artifacts as a concrete case study, validating that selective data removal is feasible while maintaining reconstruction performance on retained data. The work establishes baselines and metrics for evaluating unlearning effectiveness in medical image-to-image translation tasks.
connects: This work builds on the emerging machine unlearning literature, which has developed primarily in classification and recommendation systems, extending it to the specialized domain of medical image reconstruction. It addresses a gap between general unlearning methods and their applicability to structured image translation tasks. The paper engages with prior work on bias and artifact removal in medical imaging, leveraging the insight that unlearning can serve as a principled approach to eliminate unwanted training signal. It also connects to broader research on multi-organ or multi-dataset training in medical imaging, where dataset combination introduces new challenges. The study relates to privacy-preserving machine learning in healthcare and the need to comply with regulations like GDPR that require data removal capabilities.
enables: This work opens pathways for privacy-compliant medical imaging systems where patient data can be removed post-deployment without costly model retraining. It enables future research into selective bias mitigation in medical AI, where harmful training artifacts—such as dataset-specific biases or spurious correlations—can be surgically removed. The protocol for measuring inter-dataset knowledge transfer provides a framework for understanding which data samples have the largest influence on learned artifacts. Follow-up work could apply unlearning to remove other unwanted patterns such as demographic biases, scanner artifacts, or reconstruction failure modes. The findings also suggest applications in federated learning for medical imaging, where privacy-respecting model updates without full data retention become feasible.
